questions.question: State whether the following statement is TRUE or FALSE: There is a linear-time algorithm for testing the planarity of finite graphs.
questions.to_find: Whether planarity of a finite graph can be tested in linear time.

understand.plain: Testing whether a graph can be drawn in the plane without edge crossings sounds hard, but it can actually be done very fast. The Hopcroft-Tarjan algorithm (1974) tests planarity in O(V + E) time, which is linear in the size of the graph, and since a simple planar graph has at most 3V - 6 edges, this is just O(V). So the statement is TRUE.

keywords.explain: a graph that can be drawn on a plane so that no two edges cross
keywords.example: any tree or cycle is planar

keywords.explain: running time grows proportionally to the input size, here O(V + E)
keywords.example: Hopcroft-Tarjan planarity test runs in O(V + E)

keywords.explain: deciding whether a given graph admits a crossing-free drawing
keywords.example: checking for a K_5 or K_{3,3} minor

understand.visual_alt: Conceptually, the Hopcroft-Tarjan path-addition method processes each vertex and edge a constant number of times, giving an O(V + E) linear-time planarity test.

given.aim: Decide if the claim of a linear-time planarity test is correct.

terms.meaning: number of vertices of the graph
terms.connects: bounds the running time of the test

terms.meaning: number of edges of the graph
terms.connects: so O(V + E) collapses to O(V) for planar inputs

terms.meaning: the classic O(V + E) planarity algorithm
terms.example: uses depth-first search and path addition
terms.connects: establishes that the statement is TRUE

given.plan: Recall the known result that planarity can be decided in O(V + E) time, then conclude the statement is true.

steps.title: Recall the planarity-testing result
steps.apply: Hopcroft and Tarjan (1974) gave an O(V + E) algorithm that decides whether a graph is planar; Booth-Lueker (PQ-trees) and the left-right/Boyer-Myrvold methods are also linear time.
steps.note: linear in the number of vertices plus edges

steps.title: Why O(V + E) is effectively linear here
steps.formula_raw: for a simple connected planar graph, E <= 3V - 6
steps.apply: for the planar case E = O(V), so the test is O(V); for non-planar inputs the algorithm halts early once a forbidden structure appears.
steps.note: either way the time is linear in the input size

solution.result: A linear-time planarity test exists, so the statement is TRUE.

questions.formula_note: Key idea: the Hopcroft-Tarjan algorithm tests planarity in O(V + E) (linear) time, so the statement is TRUE.

questions.question: Which of the following graphs is/are planar? G1: a graph on 6 vertices drawn as three rows of two vertices each with the complete bipartite K_{3,3} crossing pattern. G2: the 3-cube (Q3) drawn as one square inside another with corresponding corners joined. G3: a 6-vertex graph drawn with a top apex, two upper-side vertices, two lower-side vertices and a bottom apex, with the diagonals meeting near the centre.
questions.answer: G2 and G3 are planar; G1 (= K_{3,3}) is not planar.
questions.to_find: Which of the three drawn graphs G1, G2, G3 are planar.

understand.plain: A graph is planar if it can be redrawn with no edges crossing. The crossings in the printed pictures do not matter - what matters is whether a crossing-free drawing exists. G1 is the complete bipartite graph K_{3,3}, one of the two Kuratowski graphs, so it can never be drawn without a crossing. G2 is the 3-cube Q3, which redraws cleanly as a square inside a square, and G3 redraws without crossings too, so both G2 and G3 are planar.

keywords.explain: the complete bipartite graph on 3 + 3 vertices; a forbidden Kuratowski graph that is never planar
keywords.example: the classic utilities (three houses, three utilities) graph

keywords.explain: the graph of a cube's 8 corners and 12 edges; it is planar (Schlegel diagram: square inside a square)
keywords.example: a cube redrawn as one square nested in another

keywords.explain: crossings in a given picture can sometimes be removed by moving vertices; planarity asks whether SOME crossing-free drawing exists
keywords.example: K_4 drawn with a crossing is still planar

understand.visual_svg: <svg viewBox='0 0 600 300' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' role='img' aria-label='K_3_3 is non-planar while the cube redraws planar'><title>G1 = K_{3,3} non-planar; G2 = cube class='hi'/><circle cx='490' cy='160' r='7' class='hi'/><text x='460' y='250' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ok'>square inside a square: no crossings</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: G1 is K_{3,3}, which is non-planar (every drawing has a crossing), while G2 (the cube Q3) redraws as a square inside a square and G3 also redraws without crossings, so both are planar.

given.aim: Classify each of G1, G2, G3 as planar or non-planar.

terms.meaning: the 6-vertex graph with the K_{3,3} crossing pattern
terms.example: three top vertices each joined to three bottom vertices
terms.connects: this is exactly K_{3,3}, a Kuratowski graph -> non-planar

terms.example: a square drawn inside another square

terms.meaning: the 6-vertex graph drawn with a central crossing
terms.connects: the central crossing can be removed -> planar

given.plan: Identify G1 as the Kuratowski graph K_{3,3} (never planar), then show G2 and G3 admit crossing-free redrawings.

steps.title: G1 is K_{3,3}
steps.formula_raw: a graph is planar iff it has no subgraph homeomorphic to K_5 or K_{3,3}
steps.apply: G1's three top vertices are each joined to all three bottom vertices: that is K_{3,3} itself, a forbidden Kuratowski graph, so G1 is NOT planar.
steps.note: no relabelling can remove the crossing

steps.title: G2 (cube) and G3 redraw planar
steps.formula_raw: absence of any K_5 or K_{3,3} subdivision => planar
steps.apply: The 3-cube Q3 redraws as a square inside a square (its Schlegel diagram) with no crossings, and G3's single central crossing can be undone by moving one edge outside, so both are planar.
steps.note: the printed crossings are artefacts of the drawing, not obstructions

solution.result: G2 and G3 are planar; G1 = K_{3,3} is not planar.

questions.formula_note: Key idea: by Kuratowski's theorem G1 = K_{3,3} is non-planar, while the cube G2 and the graph G3 contain no K_5 or K_{3,3} subdivision and therefore redraw without crossings.

questions.question: A graph is planar if and only if, A. It does not contain a subgraph homeomorphic to K_5 and K_{3,3}. B. It does not contain a subgraph isomorphic to K_5 and K_{3,3}. C. It does not contain a subgraph isomorphic to K_5 or K_{3,3}. D. It does not contain a subgraph homeomorphic to K_5 or K_{3,3}.
questions.answer: D. It does not contain a subgraph homeomorphic to K_5 or K_{3,3}.
questions.to_find: The correct statement of Kuratowski's planarity criterion.

understand.plain: Kuratowski's theorem says a graph is planar exactly when it has NO subgraph that is a subdivision (a homeomorphic copy) of either K_5 or K_{3,3}. Two words must be exactly right: 'homeomorphic' (not merely 'isomorphic', because the forbidden graph may appear with extra degree-2 vertices placed along its edges) and 'or' (forbidding either one is enough). Only option D has both words correct.

keywords.explain: two graphs are homeomorphic if one can be obtained from the other by inserting/deleting degree-2 vertices on edges
keywords.example: a triangle and a triangle with one edge split into two are homeomorphic

keywords.explain: K_5 (complete graph on 5 vertices) and K_{3,3} (complete bipartite) are the two minimal non-planar graphs
keywords.example: K_5 has 10 edges; K_{3,3} has 9 edges

keywords.explain: the graph must avoid K_5 OR K_{3,3} subdivisions - containing even one of them makes it non-planar
keywords.example: K_5 alone is non-planar; it need not also contain K_{3,3}

understand.visual_svg: <svg viewBox='0 0 600 270' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' role='img' aria-label='Subdivision of an edge keeps the graph homeomorphic'><title>Homeomorphic copy via subdivision</title><desc>Inserting a degree-2 vertex on an edge produces a homeomorphic graph, which is why Kuratowski's theorem must say homeomorphic and not isomorphic.</desc><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><text x='150' y='40' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>edge u - v</text><line class='edge' x1='70' y1='120' x2='230' y2='120' stroke='#B4B2A9' stroke-width='2.5'/><circle cx='70' cy='120' r='13' class='bg'/><circle cx='230' cy='120' r='13' class='bg'/><text x='70' y='160' text-anchor='middle' class='sub mut'>u</text><text x='230' y='160' text-anchor='middle' class='sub mut'>v</text><text x='300' y='125' text-anchor='middle' class='big mut'>-></text><text x='460' y='40' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>subdivided (homeomorphic)</text><line class='edge' x1='370' y1='120' x2='460' y2='120' stroke='#7F77DD' stroke-width='3'/><line class='edge' x1='460' y1='120' x2='550' y2='120' stroke='#7F77DD' stroke-width='3'/><circle cx='370' cy='120' r='13' class='bg'/><circle cx='460' cy='120' r='11' class='hi'/><circle cx='550' cy='120' r='13' class='bg'/><text x='370' y='160' text-anchor='middle' class='sub mut'>u</text><text x='460' y='160' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>deg-2</text><text x='550' y='160' text-anchor='middle' class='sub mut'>v</text><text x='300' y='235' text-anchor='middle' class='tag ink'>same graph up to subdivision: 'homeomorphic', not just 'isomorphic'</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: Subdividing an edge inserts a degree-2 vertex but yields a homeomorphic graph, which is why Kuratowski's theorem forbids homeomorphic copies (not just isomorphic ones) of K_5 OR K_{3,3} - option D.

given.aim: Pick the option that states Kuratowski's theorem correctly.

terms.meaning: isomorphic = exact same structure; homeomorphic = same up to subdividing edges
terms.example: K_{3,3} with a vertex placed on one edge is homeomorphic, not isomorphic, to K_{3,3}
terms.connects: the criterion needs 'homeomorphic', ruling out B and C

terms.meaning: 'and' would wrongly require both forbidden graphs; 'or' forbids either
terms.example: K_5 alone already makes a graph non-planar
terms.connects: the criterion needs 'or', ruling out A and B

given.plan: Match the two required words - 'homeomorphic' and 'or' - against the four options and keep the one that has both.

steps.title: Need 'homeomorphic', not 'isomorphic'
steps.formula_raw: planar  <=>  no subgraph homeomorphic to K_5 or K_{3,3}
steps.apply: Forbidden copies may carry extra degree-2 vertices on their edges, so 'isomorphic' is too narrow -> options B and C are wrong.

steps.title: Need 'or', not 'and'
steps.formula_raw: containing K_5 OR K_{3,3} (as a subdivision) destroys planarity
steps.apply: A graph with just a K_5 subdivision is already non-planar; it need not also contain K_{3,3}. So 'and' is wrong -> options A and B are out, leaving D.

questions.formula_note: Key idea: Kuratowski's theorem requires 'homeomorphic' (subdivisions count) and 'or' (either forbidden graph suffices), so the correct phrasing is option D.

questions.question: Maximum number of edges in a planar graph with n vertices is ______
questions.to_find: Largest possible edge count of a simple planar graph on n vertices.

understand.plain: Draw a simple planar graph with no crossings. Euler's formula V - E + F = 2 ties together vertices, edges and faces. Every face is bounded by at least 3 edges and each edge borders 2 faces, which forces 2E >= 3F. Combining this with Euler's formula gives the famous bound E <= 3n - 6, attained by a maximal (triangulated) planar graph.

keywords.explain: for a connected planar graph V - E + F = 2, linking vertices, edges and faces
a region of the plane bounded by edges, including the unbounded outer region
keywords.example: a triangle splits the plane into 2 faces

keywords.explain: a planar graph where every face (including the outer one) is a triangle; it attains E = 3n - 6
keywords.example: the maximal planar graph on n vertices

understand.visual_svg: <svg viewBox='0 0 560 280' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' role='img' aria-label='Maximal planar graph attains 3n-6 edges'><title>Triangulation gives E = 3n - 6</title><desc>A maximal planar graph on four vertices is the triangulated K_4 with 6 edges, matching 3 times 4 minus 6 equals 6.</desc><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><text x='280' y='35' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>K_4: every face a triangle, E = 3(4) - 6 = 6</text><line x1='280' y1='70' x2='160' y2='220' stroke='#7F77DD' stroke-width='2.5'/><line x1='280' y1='70' x2='400' y2='220' stroke='#7F77DD' stroke-width='2.5'/><line x1='160' y1='220' x2='400' y2='220' stroke='#7F77DD' stroke-width='2.5'/><line x1='280' y1='70' x2='280' y2='175' stroke='#7F77DD' stroke-width='2.5'/><line x1='160' y1='220' x2='280' y2='175' stroke='#7F77DD' stroke-width='2.5'/><line x1='400' y1='220' x2='280' y2='175' stroke='#7F77DD' stroke-width='2.5'/><circle cx='280' cy='70' r='12' class='hi'/><circle cx='160' cy='220' r='12' class='hi'/><circle cx='400' cy='220' r='12' class='hi'/><circle cx='280' cy='175' r='12' class='hi'/><text x='280' y='265' text-anchor='middle' class='tag ink'>each region triangular -> 2E = 3F -> E = 3n - 6</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: K_4 drawn as a triangulation has 6 edges = 3(4) - 6, illustrating that a maximal simple planar graph on n vertices reaches the bound E = 3n - 6.

given.aim: Find the maximum number of edges over all simple planar graphs on n vertices.

terms.meaning: number of faces in a planar embedding

given.plan: Apply Euler's formula V - E + F = 2 together with the face-edge inequality 2E >= 3F to derive E <= 3n - 6.

steps.title: Each face needs >= 3 edges
steps.apply: Every face is bounded by at least 3 edges and each edge lies on exactly 2 faces, so 2E >= 3F, i.e. F <= 2E/3.
steps.note: true for any simple planar graph with n >= 3

steps.title: Combine with Euler's formula
steps.apply: F = 2 - V + E = 2 - n + E. Substitute into F <= 2E/3: 2 - n + E <= 2E/3 -> E/3 <= n - 2 -> E <= 3n - 6.
steps.note: equality holds when every face is a triangle

questions.formula_note: Key idea: Euler's formula V - E + F = 2 with 2E >= 3F yields E <= 3n - 6, the maximum attained by a triangulation.

questions.question: A non-planar graph with minimum number of vertices has A. 9 edges, 6 vertices B. 6 edges, 4 vertices C. 10 edges, 5 vertices D. 9 edges, 5 vertices
questions.to_find: The vertex and edge count of the smallest (fewest-vertex) non-planar graph.

understand.plain: The two smallest non-planar graphs are K_5 (complete graph on 5 vertices) and K_{3,3} (on 6 vertices). The one with the FEWEST vertices is K_5, which has 5 vertices. Being complete on 5 vertices, it has C(5,2) = 10 edges. So the minimum-vertex non-planar graph has 5 vertices and 10 edges.

keywords.explain: the complete graph on 5 vertices; every pair joined; the smallest non-planar graph by vertex count
complete bipartite graph on 3 + 3 vertices; non-planar but uses 6 vertices
any graph on 4 or fewer vertices is planar, so non-planarity needs at least 5 vertices

understand.visual_svg: <svg viewBox='0 0 560 300' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' role='img' aria-label='K_5 is the smallest non-planar graph'><title>K_5: 5 vertices, 10 edges</title><desc>The complete graph K_5 on five vertices arranged as a pentagon with all diagonals has ten edges and is the smallest non-planar graph by vertex count.</desc><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><text x='280' y='35' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>K_5: 5 vertices, C(5,2) = 10 edges</text><g stroke='#7F77DD' stroke-width='2'><line x1='280' y1='80' x2='400' y2='165'/><line x1='280' y1='80' x2='355' y2='255'/><line x1='280' y1='80' x2='205' y2='255'/><line x1='280' y1='80' x2='160' y2='165'/><line x1='400' y1='165' x2='355' y2='255'/><line x1='400' y1='165' x2='205' y2='255'/><line x1='400' y1='165' x2='160' y2='165'/><line x1='355' y1='255' x2='205' y2='255'/><line x1='355' y1='255' x2='160' y2='165'/><line x1='205' y1='255' x2='160' y2='165'/></g><circle cx='280' cy='80' r='12' class='no'/><circle cx='400' cy='165' r='12' class='no'/><circle cx='355' cy='255' r='12' class='no'/><circle cx='205' cy='255' r='12' class='no'/><circle cx='160' cy='165' r='12' class='no'/><text x='280' y='290' text-anchor='middle' class='tag ink'>fewest vertices among non-planar graphs</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: K_5, the complete graph on 5 vertices drawn as a pentagon with all diagonals, has 10 edges and is the non-planar graph with the fewest vertices.

given.aim: Identify the smallest-by-vertices non-planar graph and read off its edge count.

terms.meaning: fewest vertices for which a non-planar graph exists
terms.connects: rules out 4-vertex options (all 4-vertex graphs are planar)

terms.meaning: edges of the complete graph on 5 vertices
terms.connects: gives the 10 edges in the answer

terms.meaning: to be non-planar on 5 vertices the graph must be the complete K_5
terms.example: 9 edges on 5 vertices (one edge missing) is planar
terms.connects: so 5 vertices forces exactly 10 edges, eliminating option D

given.plan: Use that the two minimal non-planar graphs are K_5 (5 vertices) and K_{3,3} (6 vertices); pick the fewer-vertex one (K_5) and count its edges with C(n,2).

steps.title: Smallest non-planar graph by vertices
steps.formula_raw: minimal non-planar graphs are K_5 (5 vertices) and K_{3,3} (6 vertices)
steps.apply: Every graph on <= 4 vertices is planar, so non-planarity first appears at 5 vertices with K_5; K_{3,3} needs 6 vertices. The minimum is K_5.

steps.title: Count edges of K_5
steps.apply: C(5,2) = 5 x 4 / 2 = 10 edges. (Dropping any edge makes K_5 minus e planar, so the 9-edge option D is planar.)

questions.formula_note: Key idea: the fewest-vertex non-planar graph is K_5 with 5 vertices and C(5,2) = 10 edges, so option C; K_{3,3} (6 vertices) has more vertices.

questions.question: Let G be a simple connected planar graph with 13 vertices and 19 edges. Then, the number of faces in the planar embedding of the graph is: A. 6 B. 8 C. 9 D. 13
questions.to_find: Number of faces F in the planar embedding of G.

understand.plain: For any connected planar graph, Euler's formula links vertices, edges and faces: V - E + F = 2. Here V = 13 and E = 19, so just solve for F: F = 2 - V + E = 2 - 13 + 19 = 8. The faces include all bounded regions plus the single unbounded outer face.

keywords.explain: V - E + F = 2 for a connected planar graph (counting the outer face)
a maximal region of the plane bounded by edges; one of them is the infinite outer region
a crossing-free drawing of a connected graph; Euler's count needs connectivity
keywords.example: the formula holds for each connected embedded graph

understand.visual_svg: <svg viewBox='0 0 560 250' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' role='img' aria-label='Euler formula V minus E plus F equals 2'><title>Euler's formula gives F = 8</title><desc>Substituting V equals 13 and E equals 19 into V minus E plus F equals 2 gives F equals 2 minus 13 plus 19 equals 8.</desc><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><rect x='40' y='50' width='480' height='60' rx='8' class='bg'/><text x='280' y='90' text-anchor='middle' class='big ink'>V - E + F = 2</text><rect x='40' y='140' width='480' height='60' rx='8' class='hi'/><text x='280' y='180' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>F = 2 - 13 + 19 = 8</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: Plugging V = 13 and E = 19 into Euler's formula V - E + F = 2 gives F = 2 - 13 + 19 = 8 faces.

given.aim: Compute the face count of a connected planar graph from V and E.
given.plan: Apply Euler's formula V - E + F = 2 and solve for F = 2 - V + E.

steps.title: Write Euler's formula
steps.apply: G is simple, connected and planar, so Euler's formula applies directly.
steps.note: valid for any connected planar embedding

steps.title: Solve for F
steps.note: the 8 faces include the outer unbounded face

questions.formula_note: Key idea: Euler's formula V - E + F = 2 gives F = 2 - 13 + 19 = 8, so the answer is option B.

formulas.name: Euler's Formula for Connected Planar Graphs
formulas.plain: for a connected planar graph drawn in the plane, vertices minus edges plus faces equals 2 (the faces include the unbounded outer face)
formulas.whenToUse: to relate vertices, edges and faces of a connected planar embedding, or to derive edge bounds like E <= 3V - 6

terms.means: number of faces, counting the outer infinite region

formulas.trap: the graph must be connected; for a planar graph with c components the formula becomes V - E + F = 1 + c. Do not forget to count the outer face.

formulas.name: Kuratowski's Theorem (Planarity Criterion)
formulas.latex: G \text{ planar} \iff G \text{ has no subgraph homeomorphic to } K_5 \text{ or } K_{3,3}
formulas.plain: a graph is planar if and only if it contains no subgraph that is a subdivision (homeomorphic copy) of K_5 or of K_{3,3}
formulas.whenToUse: to decide planarity, or to identify the minimal forbidden non-planar structures

terms.means: complete bipartite graph on 3 + 3 vertices (9 edges)
equal up to inserting/deleting degree-2 vertices on edges (a subdivision)

formulas.trap: the criterion needs 'homeomorphic' (subdivisions count, not just isomorphic copies) and 'or' (either forbidden graph is enough); using 'isomorphic' or 'and' is wrong
formulas.reference: Kuratowski (1930); equivalently Wagner's theorem uses K_5 or K_{3,3} minors
